chunk_summary: This section discusses school food regulations in India and front-of-pack labeling policies to reduce ultra-processed food (UPF) exposure and obesity risk among children, emphasizing actionable steps for clinicians and policy advocacy.
learning_objectives: Understand the 2020 Indian school food regulation restricting high-fat, high-sugar, and high-sodium foods near schools.
Evaluate the evolving status of front-of-pack labeling policies, including the Indian Nutrition Rating (INR) star-rating model.
Identify actionable advocacy strategies for clinicians to improve school food environments.
Recognize the importance of verifying current regulatory status before making policy claims.
key_facts: The 2020 Indian regulation prohibits advertising, marketing, or selling high-fat, high-sugar, or high-sodium foods within 50 meters of school campuses.
FSSAI proposed the Indian Nutrition Rating (INR) star-rating model for front-of-pack labeling in September 2022.
Debate continues over the effectiveness of star ratings versus warning-style labels for front-of-pack nutrition information.
Clinicians can advocate for removing sugar-sweetened beverages and increasing minimally processed snacks in school canteens.
Policy knowledge supports environmental changes rather than placing responsibility solely on families.
reference_quote: No person shall 'advertise or market or sell' food products high in saturated fat, trans-fat, added sugar, or sodium on the school campus or within 50 meters of the school gate in any direction. (Regulations, 2020)
